data.title: Real Estate Income Is The Wisest Choice In Uncertain Times: NNN
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

We've frequently compared the economic conditions of today to the conditions that occurred in the early 2000s. To be clear, no two time periods are identical, but sometimes history rhymes. The problem is that over 26 years, a lot of holdings today that didn't exist. This is true of our portfolio, and it's true of the S&P 500. Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), META (META), and Tesla (TSLA) didn't exist in 2000.

One holding that we have that did exist back then is NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN). Back then, it was named Commercial Net Lease Realty, but it used the same ticker.

From 1998 to 2000, NNN was super unpopular. Its share price declined 45%. Nobody wanted to invest in boring old real estate. Interest rates were high and climbing. The 10-year Treasury spiked up from a low of 4.2% in 1998 to a high of 6.7% in 2000. For real estate companies, a high 10-year Treasury rate is a headwind to earnings. After all, they use leverage to amplify returns. Meanwhile, tech was super popular. Anything Internet automatically traded at a superpremium valuation. I distinctly remember some of my peers making fun of the "old guys" who "didn't understand the Internet" and their old fogie style of investing. Everybody who liked money "knew" that buying Growth stocks was the way to go. After all, look at the charts!

Like with many things, as we age, we realize that our elders are smarter than we thought.

NNN's Dot-Com Experience

By 2001, the optimism around unlimited upside was starting to slip. There were still plenty of internet bulls around, but prices were off highs for the S&P 500. Meanwhile, REITs like NNN were up from their bottom, but still down a lot from 1998. Then the catalyst fell.

The labor market weakened, and the Fed started cutting rates. Fear and uncertainty rose in the markets, and investors were losing confidence in how companies would monetize the Internet.

In September 2001, the twin towers fell, and the U.S. was going to war in the Middle East. Fear, uncertainty, and doubt ramped up. The S&P fell some more.

What didn't fall? NNN.

In the face of a recession, a weakening economy, and war in the Middle East, NNN rallied.

It may be hard to imagine that we could be in a situation where there is a weakening labor market.

. . . and a market where there are serious questions about whether a new technology will be as profitable for companies as predicted. AI profitability is overrated - Search News

. . . and the risk of war in the Middle East?

You get our point. Yes, things are a little bit different. There is a lot of nuance, but the risks are high. Fear and doubt are rising in the markets. In 2000, that was great news for NNN investors.

Why NNN Did Well In Tough Times

The reason is simple. NNN runs a business with a straightforward concept. They buy properties and lease them. Typically, NNN will buy properties in what is called a "sale-leaseback" transaction. A tenant owns a bunch of properties, but for a corporation, owning properties isn't a good use of capital. Real estate ties up a lot of cash that could be used to fund their core business. So, they strike a deal with NNN. The REIT buys the properties from the tenant, simultaneously entering into a long-term lease. The tenant then takes the cash and invests it in their business, and pays rent to NNN. The tenant gets capital without increasing debt, NNN gets a long-term income stream and ownership of the real estate, which can be sold or leased to a new tenant in the future if the current tenant ever decides to move out.

It is a win/win deal, freeing up capital for the tenant, and providing NNN with a long-term stream of cash flow that is much higher than they would get through extending a typical mortgage that would be refinanced or paid off in five years.

NNN has a diversified portfolio, with over 400 tenants. This diversification, combined with 10+ year average lease terms, has led to a portfolio that has consistently seen very high occupancy. Source

NNN has an IG (Investment Grade) balance sheet, with very long maturities on its debt. Unlike many REITs that refinanced for five years in 2020/2021, NNN opted to push out many of its bonds to 2048 and beyond:

Where most REITs will refinance for five years and roll substantially all of their debt every five years, NNN opted to align long-term debt with its long-term leases.

The result is that NNN is fundamentally more stable today than it was in 2000.

NNN is trading with a yield near 5%, which is lower than our average target. Still, in exchange for the lower yield, we get an investment that is going to have very stable fundamentals. We can expect dividend growth regardless of what happens in the economy. Unlike many investments in the market today, NNN has been through bubble bursts, wars, high oil prices, credit crises, and more. Through it all, NNN has raised its dividend, creating a 36-year dividend raising streak.

Conclusion

The world is glued to the news of the Middle East, worrying about war and inflation. The market is focused on what it means for the price of oil, when investors should be looking for the businesses that will provide them with recurring cash flow regardless of what happens.

We don't know the future. We don't know if the conflict ends tomorrow or drags on for years. What we do know is that we want to be invested in businesses that will produce strong recurring cash flow in every environment – that will pay us dividends whether the markets go up or go down.

Real estate is an enduring asset that is capable of producing recurring cash flow. NNN is just one of many companies we are investing in that take advantage of that reality.

Forget predicting the market; build a fortress of cash flow so you don't have to worry about it. That's the Income Method.

data.title: UiPath: Why Take The Risk When There Are Better AI Options?
data.description:
I just saw a news story about the most and least shorted tech stocks, and UiPath (PATH) was among the most shorted, with a short interest of ~20%.

This caught my attention for two reasons. The first is that it made me curious to know why the market is so pessimistic, with such high conviction against UiPath. The second is that if by chance I had a conviction contrary to the market, I would believe in a short squeeze, although this is not my usual type of investment.

But I came across an unattractive case. I don't think UiPath is that classic cigar butt with deep value, but I also don't think it's an obvious value trap. But still, my rating is a sell, signaling a preference to avoid the stock (not short it) and prefer other means of riding this AI trend.

First, The Good Part of UiPath

Going straight to the Key Performance Indicators (KPI), the image below summarizes the financials and even the momentum of UiPath very well. Even though the valuation suggests more stressed financials, the KPIs suggest something else. The ARR (annualized renewal run rate) already exceeded $1.8 billion in the last quarter, with a net retention rate above 100% and double-digit growth.

What does all this mean? Well, basically, UiPath is not a dying business. There is not only a very substantial recurring revenue, but customers on average are maintaining their contracts or making larger contracts. And with double-digit growth, it also suggests they are acquiring more customers, which is true, because in the last quarter the company reached 2.56k customers with an ARR above $100k, compared to 2.29k in the same period of the previous year. Not to mention that it added 40 new customers with an ARR above $1 million in this period.

Another positive point is that being a software company and small/mid cap, a very compressed operating margin due to stock-based compensation would already be normal, but the scenario is slightly better than I expected. The non-GAAP operating margin in the last quarter was 31%, with a non-GAAP operating income of $150 million. But in GAAP, it’s still a margin of 17% and an operating income of $80 million.

And taking the opportunity to talk about valuation, the enterprise value of PATH is ~$4.2 billion. Even when being rigorous and using the operating income, the EV-to-OI is 13x, and this is with low double-digit growth for the top line and more than 100% for the operating income, which is very cheap.

So there has to be some problem with the moat or with the sustainability of this growth, at least according to the consensus view. And well, I don't think the trends are exactly bad for UiPath.

Even though there is a risk of disruption that I will address in the next section, there is the good part, which is a practically infinite demand for automation services through AI. UiPath already provides automation services even with AI agents, and this is a clear trend; demand will not be lacking. More and more medium-sized companies will not only want to gain efficiency and implement this type of automation, but will need to do so to stay in the market.

If we think of the Jevons Paradox, the cheaper tokens and this type of implementation become, not to mention that, as AI becomes more useful, more and more companies will want to implement this in their companies.

Pricing Headwinds and Disruption of AI Agents

I believe the biggest risk is not exactly the volume of customers or a stagnating demand for UiPath. The biggest problem, perhaps, is pricing power in the future. We have increasingly larger companies in this type of solution, Microsoft (MSFT) already has bundles offering similar services, and it has enough scale to charge something marginal for it.

Returning to the Jevons Paradox, demand ends up increasing, but in this analogy, UiPath is practically the commodity that is being cheapened. Microsoft is not the only competitive threat; in fact, there are numerous threats and alternatives. AI companies, such as Anthropic, which is doing very well in the enterprise, are a huge threat. Companies that do not want to pay more could simply try to make a proprietary AI agent with Anthropic (ANTHRO), OpenAI (OPENAI), OpenClaw, and similar.

And for those that pay a traditional consulting firm to do this, there is also no guarantee that UiPath will continue to benefit. Today many consulting companies implement UiPath, which the company calls GTM Partners (Go-to-Market), you can think of it as practically a distribution method. But how “loyal” could these consulting companies (such as the big 4) be to UiPath? In my view, it is a relationship that ends up being interesting, but in practice does not have such great value if a disruption is to occur. With more and more alternatives, these consultancies will prioritize the most practical alternative, most useful for the customer, most profitable, with more features, more security, and the chance of this being a “non-UiPath” alternative is high in the coming years.

So in this quite complex landscape, I think UiPath comes out disadvantaged. Perhaps not as a complete disruption, but with a certain difficulty in continuing to grow so rapidly, and mainly, difficulties in maintaining pricing power and even a high switching cost (because currently AI agents are very good with adaptation regardless of the system).

The Icing On The Cake For The Sell Rating

For me, a sell does not always mean that I think the stock is “a short.” As I mentioned at the beginning, I don't think UiPath is a clear value trap. But I also don't think it's such a classic case of deep value, with such an optimistic scenario for a very large shareholder yield, and above all, that it compensates for the risk.

And the icing on the cake for the sell rating, in my view, is the comparison with other alternatives. When we are talking about AI trends, we have names like the already mentioned Microsoft, trading at a forward P/E close to ~22x. We have Amazon (AMZN) with a valuation also below average, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), with a valuation a little above average but with numerous optionalities and incredible moats that allow us to be very optimistic about the next 10 years of growth through AI features. Not to mention other software companies with moats and scales larger than UiPath.

Therefore, the UiPath case ends up offering a small discount, while carrying a much higher risk than these.

data.title: Microsoft's Selloff Is Hiding A Bigger Opportunity
data.description:
The reasons why I find Microsoft (MSFT) so attractive right now are neither the size, the brand, nor even the clear AI positioning. It's the contrast between many positive trends that appear at once and the correction of the share price as if something is wrong here. This discrepancy is what I am mostly interested in.

In no way do I believe that the market doesn't grasp what is happening at Microsoft. Quite the contrary, everyone understands how strong Azure is growing, how popular Copilot is and how strategically important Microsoft is in enterprise AI adoption. What I see the problem is not in the end goal, but in reaching it.

Investors understand that all the investments Microsoft makes are likely to pay off eventually. However, there is no doubt that it will take some time for the company to turn these investments into solid bottom line results, and the market does not want to give it this time.

Why Is CapEx So Undervalued?

Microsoft's capex rose from $44.5 billion in FY2024 to $64.6 billion in FY2025, indicating over 3x growth in just four years. The increase didn't slow down there either. In Q1 FY2026, capex stood at $34.9 billion, followed by $37.5 billion in Q2. If such an investment pace continues, the annual capex will come out at roughly $140-$150 billion by the end of FY2026. While we may see a slowdown going forward, the pace of investments that management chose is without precedent for the firm.

FCF paints a different picture from capex. FY2025 FCF came out at $71.6 billion, meaning that capex started getting close to free cash flow run rate. In Q2 alone, FCF turned out at just $5.9 billion due to intense investments. There is an area in which, in my opinion, the business and the market are out of sync. This is capital expenditure. The overall reaction from the market on it is rather negative and it is not really unexpected. Capital expenditures grow rapidly and are not yet offset by cash flow. At first glance, it is a classic case for multiples' compression.

As a matter of fact, the assumption implicit in such thinking is that capex must lead to immediate revenues from Azure. Yet, the situation is a bit more complicated. Microsoft allocates computing power to several revenue generating layers which include Copilot, GitHub, internal model development and platform capability.

It affects the way returns should be evaluated in this case. There is no point discussing whether a dollar invested in capex will lead to $1 worth of Azure revenue the following year. The point is whether it will support a whole stack of various services that will multiply over time. If a GPU serves as a foundation for multiple revenues during its entire life, returns should be seen from a longer perspective.

Almost all the infrastructure Microsoft builds today is already committed or associated with demand. It definitely does not mean that there is no risk anymore. However, there is no need to believe Microsoft is creating extra capacity with no plan to use it. For me, the crucial question here is how long it takes to show in financials.

Beyond Azure: The Changing Dynamics of AI Monetization

Now, I realize that it may not be correct to limit oneself only to Azure. The growth of Azure itself is definitely the driving factor but the whole picture may be much wider as far as AI monetization is concerned. In other words, the number of paying seats is not crucial here, what matters is the potential for monetizing ARPU expansion.

Another aspect that attracts my attention is GitHub. In a sense, it is very similar to Microsoft 365 Copilot. Developers change their workflows because of AI which means that Microsoft has to build a product ecosystem. To put it bluntly, GitHub Copilot is a great example of how AI could be introduced into regular work. Again, this is not an additional activity but an integral part of the product ecosystem.

Finally, it's the enterprise perspective that is extremely important. Microsoft owns the identity layer, productivity environment and the data that drives it all. In other words, it has an opportunity to make AI not only powerful but also contextually relevant which gives it a competitive advantage.

Does the Market Ignore That?

I don't think that is the case, the market is just not ready yet to give any valuation premium for Microsoft's strategic advantages, which are yet to materialize in terms of cash flow. In my view, MSFT looks undervalued. The implied earnings multiple suggests that investors expect Microsoft's free cash flows to grow slowly over the next few years, while it does not credit the future value of present investments in full.

Considering the current price-to-future cash flow ratio, I see two basic assumptions behind the current valuation. Firstly, the company will continue spending a lot of money in capex and experience low free cash flow growth over the next period of time. Secondly, its Copilot monetization process will continue gradually. There are no big breakthroughs expected soon.

Given the current state of things, I believe that FCF gives us a better indication than earnings. Given that it generates an operating cash flow of $136 billion and a FCF of $70 billion while the quarterly capital expenditures are currently at $37.5 billion, it is evident that there has been some sort of pressure. Given that the capex intensity is forecast to peak in the near future, the current P/FCF of ~17 does not appear to be overly stretched. So, I’m comfortable with the valuation but only if I assume the issue is timing, not execution.

The Risks

I believe that the risk I should be most worried about is the failure to convert capex into returns. If it continues to grow and free cash flows will remain low with no signs of monetization, it will make the market skeptical about the business.

Another important variable is Azure growth, which is expected to slow down significantly without a compelling explanation related to the shortage of supply. Also, Copilot adoption rate should increase in order to be followed by sustained usage of its features, otherwise monetization would hardly be possible. Finally, the OpenAI relationship may change somehow in the near future. Right now, I don't see it as a risk but I would watch it carefully.

Bottom Line

In my opinion, Microsoft is a company that operates well yet is valued for its potential. The basics are fine, although there needs to be more proof from the market regarding the conversion of the capital expenditure of $37.5 billion in a quarter into cash flow.

data.title: EVN: Investment Grade Muni Bonds, Federally Tax Free
data.description:
I rate Eaton Vance Municipal Income Trust (EVN) a buy for income investors in the higher tax brackets, looking for bonds that are exempt from regular federal income tax. The closed end fund (CEF) currently yields 5.9% on an annual basis, paid in monthly distributions. The fund is currently trading at a -2.43% discount to net asset value (NAV), representing a good value for new investors. For an investor paying 30% taxes on income, EVN nets an 8.43% taxable equivalent yield. Of course it is meant for taxable accounts, not retirement accounts such as IRAs/401(k)s.

Looking forward, EVN is an attractive investment for the following reasons:

  • Interest rates are expected to be on hold or possibly drop by the end of the year.
  • Produces an 8-9% taxable equivalent yield with higher credit quality bonds.
  • Currently pays a stable monthly distribution of $0.0513, yielding 5.9% annually.
  • Goes ex-dividend 4/14.
  • Utilizes a total leverage ratio of 40% to enhance yield.
  • Active management outperforms peers in the sector.

Full disclosure, I own this closed end fund and reinvest the dividends on a monthly basis to build a tax efficient income vehicle, free from federal taxes. Assuming I pay around 30% taxes on AGI, the tax equivalent yield is higher than many of my other CEFs, such as Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) paying 7.1%, or Cohen & Steers REIT and Preferred and Income Fund (RNP) at 8.2%, one of my favorite tax efficient funds, Eaton Vance Tax Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT) which yields 7.95%.

Eaton Vance Is a Trusted Sponsor

Eaton Vance is owned by Morgan Stanley and provides advanced investing and wealth management solutions to financial advisors and high-net-worth individuals. It has a long history of providing quality closed end funds, with long histories of outperformance. I last covered some of my favorite funds in the article “Top 10 Income Funds from Eaton Vance (November Update).”

EVN—Investment Grade Bonds, Federally Tax-Free

The fund's investment objective is to provide current income exempt from regular federal income tax. The inception date of the fund was 1/29/1999. As a closed-end fund, it can trade at a discount or premium to net asset value. There are currently 241 municipal bonds in the portfolio.

The top 10 holdings account for 17.77% of the portfolio, which means that there is very little concentration risk. If one or more bond were to default, it wouldn’t make much of an impact on the fund. However, municipal bonds rarely default. As seen in the chart below, the holdings include major cities and state activities behind typical municipal projects such as airports, education, and water.

The top sectors of the fund are led by transportation at 14.1% of the fund, senior living at 11.3%, Industrial at 10.4%, and General Obligation at 10.3%.

The majority of the credit quality is investment grade, with A-AAA accounting for 57.9% of the portfolio. This indicates safety of principal, albeit at lower rates vs. the high-yield/high-risk funds.

Fund Expenses Are Higher, But Includes Leverage

Management expenses of the fund are currently less than one percent at 0.87%. The other 1.45% is the interest expense for leverage. As the portfolio manager borrows to buy bonds, as long as the bond pays more than the cost of the loan, the manager can increase the performance of the fund. If, however, the bond underperforms the cost of funds, the fund performance can sink more quickly.

Taxable Equivalent Yield is Attractive for High Income Earners

Here are the tax brackets for 2025. Assuming an investor is paying a tax rate of 32% or more, this investment has a yield of 8-9%, which is beneficial to the investor. For lower incomes or retirement accounts, the tax savings are not beneficial.

The table below shows how the annual yield of EVN translates into tax equivalent yield. For example, an investor paying a tax rate of 32% has a tax equivalent yield of 8.68% with this municipal fund.

EVN Outperforms the Municipal Bond Index

An investment of $10,000 in EVN since inception would have grown to $43,500, which outperforms the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, growing to just $26,610.

Trading At a Discount to NAV

As seen in the chart below, EVN is currently trading at a discount of -2.43% to NAV. While this isn’t as compelling as the discount of -15% that occurred in 2023, it still represents a value.

In a comparison to peers, EVN has one of the lowest expense ratio of less than one percent, with higher turnover.

Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ) has the highest AUM in the chart with over $1.2 billion. NMZ has the second highest dividend yield of 7.6%, with the most holdings and the highest expense ratio.

Invesco Investment Grade Municipal Bond (VGM) has the highest yield at 7.7%, with the strongest 5-year dividend growth rate and the lowest turnover. These metrics make it appealing vs. peers.

Eaton Vance Municipal Bond Fund (EIM) is less appealing than EVN because of its lower yield at 3.6%, higher expense ratio, and lower dividend growth rate.

An investment of $10,000 in any of these funds would have grown over time despite the 2022 decline. NMZ in particular stands out as a winner, being valued at $13,290 over that time.

Looking Ahead

Investors will always be on the lookout for ways to reduce taxes. EVN offers an opportunity to take a portion of their portfolio and invest in municipal bonds, being free of federal taxes. The discount to NAV is not as high as it has been in the past, but with calls for the Federal Reserve to cut rates in the future, the demand for a CEF paying a taxable equivalent yield of over 8% is high.

Risks/Negatives

The largest risk to owning this municipal bond CEF, is rising interest rates, which would lower the NAV, creating a loss vs. cost basis. Under the current circumstances with the Iran War underway, it is possible that the oil shock will create higher costs, therefore higher inflation, which could lead to rising interest rates later in the year. The Fed is under pressure from the President to lower rates right now, but rising inflation that grows faster than unemployment may create a need for the Fed to hike.

Summary

I rate Eaton Vance Municipal Income Trust (EVN) as a buy for income investors in the higher tax brackets, especially over 30%. As the fund is currently paying a monthly distribution yielding 5.9% annually, an investor in the 30%+ tax bracket would be realizing a tax equivalent yield of 8-9%. Looking forward, this is a good fund to own during stable interest rates, or even in a declining interest rate environment.

Eaton Vance is a trusted closed end fund provider owned by Morgan Stanley. In my opinion, the Eaton Vance funds are conservatively managed and tend to produce a high total return over time. This fund includes investment grade bonds, which reduces risk and ensures preservation of principal.

Risks to the fund include a rise in interest rates caused by inflation. This risk is not to be taken lightly due to the war in Iran, which will raise the price of oil long term. Assuming the war is complete within the next few weeks, the Fed has more pressure to lower vs. raise rates to stimulate the housing market. Therefore, I remain bullish on EVN for federal tax-free income.

data.title: LATAM Airlines: Can't Escape Higher Jet Fuel Costs (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Prior to the Iran war and the doubling of jet fuel prices, LATAM Airlines (LTM) was one of my top picks in the sector due to a benign competitive landscape, aggressive fleet expansion, appreciating Latin American currencies, and stable, low fuel prices. Today, this has changed. If jet fuel prices do not decline in 2H26, the companies' hedges will reset at a higher price, causing a sharp reduction in margins that is not incorporated into the share price. I downgrade LTM from Buy to Sell and introduce a $46 price target.

Guidance and Consensus

At the end of 2025, the company had about 40% of its fuel consumption hedged at the US$90 BOE level and used this as part of its guidance to achieve $1.7 billion in FCF and an EBITDA margin of 27%. They also expect to increase ASK (available seat kilometer) by 8-10% with the purchase of 40 aircraft for $1.7 billion in capex. This is an aggressive growth and spending plan built on expectations of cheap oil, strong local currencies, and improving macro growth across the region, as well as benign competition. Azul, Gol/Avianca are all focused on cash flow after exiting Chapter 11, not on growth or price wars.

The consensus forecasts revenue growth of 11% and an EBITDA margin of 27% for 2026, valuing the shares at $70 with an EV/EBITDA price target of 6x. Very much in line with guidance and global peers. The market has not taken into account the potential impact of fuel costs on the business model. It's very likely that LTM revises this guidance in the 1Q26 results report, or even preempts it with an earnings warning.

Jet Fuel Price Impact

LTM has 40% of 2026 fuel consumption hedged, locked in at about $90 BOE or $2.7 per gallon, which should safeguard margins through 2Q26. However, jet fuel prices are now near $200 BOE or $5 a gallon, and unless they decline back to pre-Iran war levels in 3 months, fuel costs will jump by 80% in 3Q26 and 54% in 4Q26 under my assumption for a gradual price decline over 12 months if the conflict ends by June 2026 and the production and flows resume. The airline is most likely to take steps to reduce capacity by delaying new aircraft deliveries or reducing planned new routes or frequencies, as it tries to raise prices to offset fuel costs. However, this is difficult and will likely impact demand and load factors. There is little the airline can do to mitigate this shock in oil price without impacting margins or demand; this is the Achilles heel of most airlines. Pricing and demand dynamics are made worse by currency devaluations, as most consumers earn in local currencies and airline operations are primarily in USD.

Under my estimates for the next two years, I assume LTM is hit with higher fuel costs, sticks to the low end of capacity increase, raises average yield/price by 5%, and is hurt by a lower load factor. The combination may drive EBITDA down by 28% in 2026, before a recovery in 2027 as jet fuel costs decline towards $100 BOE or $3/gallon.

Valuation

I continue to value LTM at 6x EV/EBITDA, in line with global peers. The company has executed a successful turnaround post-pandemic and through Chapter 11, attaining higher margins, capturing market share, and reducing debt and debt costs. At the same time, the distressed asset funds that emerged as the new controlling shareholders have reduced their stake and increased LTM free float, improving daily share liquidity and promoting index inclusion. However, the Iran conflict and a rise in jet fuel costs should have a negative impact on margins that will be difficult to offset. The new YE26 price target of $46 calls for 8% downside and prompts a change to the rating from Buy to Sell. If/when the Iran conflicts end and jet fuel prices begin to normalize, it could trigger a reversal of the sell view.

Peer Comps

The peer comps table below that, which uses consensus data, suggests that analysts have not yet lowered estimates or valuations due to the doubling of jet fuel costs. Many airlines do not hedge fuel costs and buy in the spot market; they will feel the brunt of price increases first and may also raise passenger fares sooner rather than later. The few that do hedge may have a strategic advantage and can gain some market share, but ultimately, if jet fuel prices do not normalize by June 2026, the whole sector will be affected.

Risk

The main risk to the sell rating is if jet fuel prices decline back to pre-Iran war levels by the end of June 2026. LTM's fuel hedges should reduce costs in 1H26, and if fuel prices revert, the company would be saved from a significant increase in costs, and my estimates and valuation would be underestimated.

Conclusion

I downgrade LTM from buy to sell. While LATAM Airlines is now a far better airline than pre-pandemic, with lower leverage, greater fleet productivity, and earnings power, it may not escape the negative impact of higher jet fuel costs in 2H26, which could lead to a drop in earnings and cash flow, making the shares overvalued in the short term. The steep rise in oil and fuel prices caused by the Iran war may last far longer than expected, given the damage to infrastructure, supply chains, and inventories. Caution is required in a highly sensitive business such as the airline industry.

data.title: MarketAxess: Upside Relies On The Turnaround
data.description:
MarketAxess (MKTX) operates a platform for trading fixed-income securities. The platform connects buyers, sellers, and dealers by different trading protocols. Its asset-light business creates high margins and returns on capital. MarketAxess has a strong net cash balance sheet that enables it to invest for growth and return capital to shareholders in the form of growing dividends and buybacks. MarketAxess' financial performance is enhanced by the oligopolistic nature of the electronic trading and protected by significant network effects.

After a steep 70% price decline and dropping from the S&P 500, MarketAxess' valuation has normalized to an attractive level. In a multi-year timeframe, MarketAxess’ share price has diverged from its main listed rival, Tradeweb Markets (TW). The management has acknowledged market share loss, taken corrective measures, and there are initial signs that the actions are working.

A platform in a growth market

MarketAxess collects fees from matching buyers and sellers of debt securities. On its platform there are available corporate debt, emerging market debt, eurobonds, municipal bonds, and U.S. government bonds. It is a market leader in corporate debt and a challenger in other classes of debt. MarketAxess believes its competitive advantage comes from its global network of more than two thousand institutional investors, dealers, and a thousand liquidity providers, and the data the platform creates.

There are several ways to trade debt on MarketAxess’ platform. In summary, there’s a different trading protocol for different needs and markets. The traditional way is to place an RFQ to selected dealers in order for them to quote on a specific debt. It has several protocols for dealers to conduct trades; the newest is the so-called Mid-X. Portfolio trading means placing a larger selection of different debt for sale. Over the years, portfolio trading has taken share from the traditional and dealer initiated protocols.

MarketAxess reported Q4 results in the beginning of February. In 2025 the revenue grew 3% to $846 million. Total expenses increased 6%. Earnings per share stood at $7.39 with a growth of 1.5%. Annual free cash flow was strong and grew from $328 million to $347 million. Profitability, measured by the EBITDA margin, decreased slightly from 50.2% to 49.7%. MarketAxess’ main rival, Tradeweb, grew its revenue by 18.9% and earnings per share by 18.8% as well.

Turnaround with early signs of success

Over the past couple of decades, MarketAxess has ridden the trend of fixed income trading moving from manual execution to electronic platforms. Still today, a larger part of the trades are done manually, providing MarketAxess an important driver of future growth. The company has grown revenue at a 12% CAGR and earnings at a 14% CAGR for the past ten years with massive but declining margins.

MarketAxess has numerous other growth drivers. It targets annual revenue growth of 8-9%. The company has renewed much of its offering and user experience in order to reverse its market share development. Its new trading protocols have achieved promising growth of volumes, and the company is going to invest $65-70 million in further development.

MarketAxess is a leader in the trading of emerging market debt. The company sees emerging market debt as a major growth pocket. It is as large a category as U.S. credit, and the penetration of electronic trading is only approximately 10%. The premise provides the company a future growth avenue, and the management expects most of its future growth to come outside of the U.S. markets. It is notable that Tradeweb is also growing rapidly internationally.

Finally, the company needs to strengthen its home base. In 2025, its revenue increased 10% excluding U.S. credit, even if U.S. credit volumes were record high. MarketAxess pursues growth in the U.S. credit market, where its market share is comparatively low. The company posted impressive volume growth, by increasing portfolio trading by 48%, dealer-initiated trading by 33%, and block trading by 24%.

The divergence of stock prices of MarketAxess and Tradeweb is largely a result of a few missteps that MarketAxess has taken. It has not invested enough in portfolio trading and dealer-to-dealer channels. Competitors have taken advantage of a change in the marketplace as portfolio and block trading have increased significantly. Furthermore, competitors have also come to eat MarketAxess’ lunch in corporate debt.

In response, MarketAxess has introduced so-called X-Pro, which has taken off rapidly. In 2025, portfolio trading grew 48%, and in January, MarketAxess realized portfolio trading rose 126%. Monthly figures fluctuate heavily, and in February, portfolio trading volume rose only 5%. Its competitors captured market share due to the high volume of new issuance. Where a privately-held company, Trumid, is strong. It grew twice as fast as the market.

The downside of the increase in portfolio and block trading is that they are less profitable than other protocols. It collects a lower fee per million (FPM) when customers utilize these protocols. In the fourth quarter, commission revenue increased 4% thanks to higher volume but was offset by the fee capture.

The risk here is that MarketAxess dilutes its profitability in a conquest to get back market share. However, that is likely necessary in order to stay relevant, as Tradeweb has been able to leverage its leading position in treasuries to attract volume in other types of debt. In addition to Tradeweb, Trumid has been growing and taking market share as well. Therefore, the success or failure of new protocols is a major determinant of MarketAxess' future.

AI is likely to increase the attractiveness of electronic trading by making pricing and matching of buyers and sellers more efficient. Algorithmic trading is increasing rapidly too, which is likely to increase customer lock-in to their selected platform. The purpose of the marketplace, such as MarketAxess, is to provide efficient trading solutions to all involved, and technology risk is ever-present.

With a market capitalization of only $6 billion, MarketAxess is much smaller than some of its peers and competitors. The size, market position, and historically depressed share price could make MarketAxess a potential buyout candidate. Players such as Nasdaq, Intercontinental Exchange, and CME Group could be potential buyers whose offerings would be complemented by MarketAxess’ scope of business.

Valuation rests on the turnaround

MarketAxess is trading at historically low multiples. The investor sentiment likely and partly hinges on market share development and profit margins, as its performance is at a historically low level too. The margins have been in a slow decline for several years.

The shares trade at a forward P/E-multiple of 20x, while the 5-year average is 37.6x. P/FCF-multiple stands at 15x, also significantly lower than the historical average. In light of recent financial performance, the valuation isn't necessarily justified. An investor needs to believe that the actions the company has taken will propel the business back to growth.

Analysts are estimating 10-13% earnings growth for this year and the two following years. Historically, MarketAxess has grown earnings 14% on average. If MarketAxess were to grow at a pace of 10% and later slow down to 7%, the fair value of the shares would be around $200. The business and industry profile could justify a lower discount rate of 8% and a P/E multiple of 20x. If the discount rate were two percentage points higher, the fair value today would be $184.

MarketAxess' peers are trading at higher multiples justified by better growth and profitability. MarketAxess appears slightly undervalued only compared to CBOE Global Markets based on the figures in the table below, although CBOE has been able to deliver an impressive jump in net income. MarketAxess's share price has declined nearly 60% over the past three years, while the peers have delivered over 50% total returns.

Company

MarketAxess

MarketAxess is followed by 13 analysts with an average target price of $193.7.

Significant buybacks and a modest dividend

MarketAxess pays a quarterly dividend of $0.78 resulting in a dividend yield of 1.9%. The dividend has increased 16 years consecutively at an annual pace of 14%. However, the latest dividend increase was only 3%. The payout ratio stands comfortably at around 41%.

In addition to the dividend, the company has a buyback program of $505 million over the next couple of years. There is $205 million left under the program. In 2025 MarketAxess bought back nearly two million shares at an average price of $181. At the end of the year, the shares outstanding were 1.5% lower than the year before.

Conclusion

MarketAxess operates in a competitive but oligopolistic market, which has enabled it to grow very profitably over the years. Due to its own missteps, that growth story is fractured, leading to poor share performance compared to its peers. First signs of corrective actions are visible, and its market share turnaround could be in the early innings. An investor should pay attention to the volume growth of both MarketAxess and Tradeweb over the following quarters.

data.title: CION Investment: Deepest Discount Since Inception But Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
Overview

Business development companies continue to face headwinds related to the higher interest rate environment and it appears that CION Investment Corp (CION) is no exception. When I previously covered CION, I issued a hold rating because there were some material improvements within the portfolio. Therefore, I believe that we are getting close to a point where opportunistic investors may take advantage of the deeply discounted share price. However, CION's share price has fallen by more than 30% since my last coverage as it seems that a selloff followed CION's Q4 earnings. Therefore, I wanted to revisit CION and reassess its overall value proposition at this time.

Following the pullback in share price, CION now trades at one of its deepest discount to NAV valuations since its inception. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that CION now trades at a discount to NAV of 50.80%. For reference, CION has traded at an average discount to NAV of 31.17% over the last five year period. While entering at this large discount level may be enticing for some, I believe it is a reflection of the challenges that CION is currently facing at this time.

After reviewing the latest earnings report, it is clear that total investment income is likely to continue declining over the next few quarters. While this has been temporarily offset by lower costs in the most recent quarter, CION will eventually have to make significant improves in the rate of new investment activity so that growth can occur. CION currently offers a starting dividend yield of 17.7% and issues its payouts on a monthly basis. Although net investment income still supports payouts, I am cautious about the possibility of a dividend cut if earnings do not improve over the next few quarters.

Q4 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio overview, CION now has total investments at a fair value of $1.69B that are spread across a diverse range of debt securities. One of the main challenges for the portfolio is the lack of growth catalysts over the last year. CION has experienced sales and repayments that continue to outpace new growth initiatives, so the fair value of the portfolio has declined from the prior year of $1.82B. While the value has declined, CION's total debt outstanding has actually increased over the last year, up to $1.14B. This indicates that CION is becoming more reliant on debt to fuel its operations.

The portfolio relies most heavily on first lien debt investments, which is ideal because this form of investment sits at the top of the corporate capital structure. This means that these debt investments have the highest priority for repayment and this reduces the risk that CION loses all invested capital in bad deal. The portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • First Lien: 80.8%
  • Equity: 18.5%
  • Unsecured Debt: 0.4%
  • Collateralized Securities: 0.3%

Additionally, CION aims to maintain an efficient allocation across different industries. The investment leans most heavily on Services: Business at 14.7% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to healthcare and retail investments, which account for 11.3% and 11% of assets respectively. Notably, CION only has software exposure totaling 1.8% of assets and this is reassuring during a time where the market is pessimistic towards SaaS companies. The rapid growth and innovations in AI has investors questioning if SaaS companies can maintain their moat. So CION's limited exposure here may be working to its benefit.

CION reported its Q4 earnings in mid-March and the results were mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.35 per share, which slightly beat expectations by $0.01. Total investment income amounted to $53.79M for the quarter, which was a decline from the prior year's total investment income of $57.89M. The biggest driver of the decline was a reduction in interest income received from portfolio companies. In fact, CION's interest income now sits at its lowest levels ever at $43.99M for the quarter, which was a large reduction from the prior year's total investment income of $51.36M.

To help offset the lower investment income totals, CION was able to cut some costs and reduce its operating expenses. For instance, total expenses before taxes landed at $35.49M, which was a reduction from the prior year's expenses of $39.08M. Management fees slightly reduced but the biggest reduction in costs comes from the lower interest and other debt expenses for the quarter. It would be a significant improvement in portfolio health if CION is capable of reducing its debt during this time of higher interest rates.

Higher interest rates can also make it difficult for underlying positions to rise in value. Since the majority of CION's positions are structured on a floating rate basis, a higher interest rate environment directly increases its interest expenses and shrink operating margins. Tighter operating spreads for borrowers can make it very difficult to grow earnings. This is likely why CION experienced a large net change in unrealized depreciation of $59.53M for the quarter, while only capturing a small $118K in net realized gains.

The good news is that CION doesn't have much debt maturing during 2026, which provides some runway for conditions to improve. CION has $1.14B in total principal outstanding at an average interest rate of 7.35%. CION does have $115M worth of debt maturing this year, but most of its debt will be maturing throughout 2027.

Outlook & Risk Profile

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CION has struggled to hold onto any positive momentum. For instance, CION's share price has declined by 35.4%, with most of that declining happening as we crossed into 2026. Even when including all distributions paid, the total return sits at a loss of nearly 25.2% over the same time frame. Unfortunately, I don't think that performance is likely to improve over the next quarter when looking at the lack of growth within the portfolio.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $9.17 per share, which indicates a potential upside of ~35.4% from its current levels. However, I believe this is very optimistic and I do not see any growth catalysts that can allow the share price to experience this sort of double-digit upside anytime soon. Unfortunately, it seems like the outlook of CION ultimately comes down to which direction interest rates move. The Fed decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their latest meeting, which means the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. With rising geopolitical tensions, an uptick in unemployment, and uncertainty around technology, the Fed may decide to wait on the sidelines until macro conditions improve.

High interest rates have proven to be a challenge for CION since it drastically limits the amount of borrowers seeking debt financing. If management has trouble finding new opportunities to invest in, I anticipate that CION's underlying NAV will continue to erode through 2026. For instance, CION was able to allocate $66M towards new funded investments, while another $12M came from the fundings of previously unfunded commitments. However, this progress was offset by repayments totaling $79M for the quarter, which translated to a negative net funded investment activity of $1M.

As the NAV continues to deteriorate, the share price will also decline and cause investor capital to erode. Ideally, lower interest rates can offset these challenges as it would provide relief to existing borrowers and increase the likelihood of new borrowers flooding the market. Over the last year CION's NAV has continued to decline, despite a brief change of course in mid-2025. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts over the last year:

  • Q4 2024: $15.43 per share
  • Q1 2025: $14.28 per share
  • Q2 2025: $14.50 per share
  • Q3 2025: $14.86 per share
  • Q4 2025: $13.76 per share

Even when assessing CION's own internal risk ratings, we can see that the portfolio companies are facing trouble. The internal rating system considers a '1-grade' as the highest form of borrower where there is the least amount of risk to their cost basis. Conversely, a '5-grade' represents the lowest quality borrower that has the highest risk. Last quarter, 87.2% of CION's portfolio was classified as a 1 or 2. In this most recent quarter, only 86.1% of portfolio companies fell within the 1 or 2 rating range. Furthermore, we've seen an uptick in the portfolio companies that fall within the rating range of 3-5.

On the latest earnings call, management confirmed that they added one new non-accrual to their portfolio. However, non-accruals slightly increased to 1.78% of fair portfolio value, up from 1.75% of fair portfolio value last quarter. Despite non-accruals remaining quite low, this doesn't provide us with enough info to closely assess the risk profile of the portfolio.

This increase was from the addition of one new name to nonaccrual, our term loan investment in HW acquisition or HealthWay. HealthWay initiated a primary revolver raise in the fourth quarter that ultimately funded in early 2026 and contained a substantial lower component that effectively shifted value from the term loan to the revolver tranche. - Gregg Bresner, President

I believe that another metric to pay attention to is the amount of PIK (paid-in-kind) interest income that CION relies on. PIK interest income is a measure of investment income that has been measured through the issuance of additional debt, rather than actual cash flow. This is a way that allows portfolio companies to delay cash payments until conditions improve and it can be an indication that CION's positions are struggling to keep up with payments. The latest 10-K form indicates that CION has categorized ~$49.2M as PIK interest income for the year of 2025. This is an increase from the prior year's PIK interest income of ~$43M, which I believe is a sign that there is more downside risk for investors.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.10 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 17.7%. Converting this monthly distribution to a quarterly payout, we can get a $0.30 per share distribution every quarter. Believe it or not, this massive dividend yield is still supported by net investment income of $0.35 per share for the quarter. This indicates that net investment income currently supports distributions at a coverage rate of 117%. Therefore, CION is likely to keep payouts the same for the rest of 2026, assuming that there aren't any material losses experienced within the portfolio.

I would still remain cautious since the scope of the BDC market can change quite rapidly. Any spike in non-accruals can result in net investment income falling below the distribution level. Furthermore, CION's struggle to invest capital into new opportunities can also be a problem if repayments continue to outpace these new investments, which can also cause earnings to trend lower. So while the distributions are supported by earnings, it is still challenging to recommend a position in CION for income investors seeking stability.

Historically, income received from BDCs did not offer any tax-efficiency so investors were best off utilizing these positions in a tax-advantaged account. However, some changes as a result of the One Big Beautiful Bill may help BDC investors. This bill allows 'qualified' production activities and pass-through income structures to receive a discount on the effective tax rate. It will essentially allow the distributions from BDCs to have a similar classification as the dividends received from REITs. However, I would still hold a BDC in a tax-advantaged account to avoid any unnecessary tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that CION remains a hold at this time. While the earnings are still technically supporting distributions, I think it is very unlikely that we see any meaningful growth over the next quarter. Repayments continue to outpace the rate of new investment activity, which indicates that the NAV may continue to erode over the next quarter. Furthermore, CION has seen its PIK interest income rise over the last year and this can be an indication that the portfolio companies are struggling to keep up with payments. So although CION trades at a massive discount to NAV, I don't think we've seen the bottom yet. Looking forward, I think a lower interest rate environment will be the primary driver of recovery efforts.

data.title: Spok Holdings: Software Gains Are Still Too Low To Offset Structural Declines
data.description:
Introduction

Spok Holdings’ (SPOK) share value has been cratering this past year, so I thought it would be a great time to revisit it, since I haven’t covered it since October of 2024, when I gave it a hold rating, as there was no rush to start a position. I came to the same conclusion this time around as well. The wireless business will continue to decline, which will affect its cash flow, and the software side of things is taking much too long to offset the structural declines in legacy.

Briefly on performance

Starting from the very top, we can see that the company’s revenue growth stalled in the second half of the year. This is primarily due to the legacy wireless business continually shrinking, while its software booking came in rather choppy as well. The new software gains were just not enough to keep the total revenue accelerating. Back in Q2 of the year, the management was confident enough to raise full-year guidance, but it turns out it was a bit premature, as the second half of the year was softer overall. By Q4, software bookings bounced back sharply, but the damage of H2 was already done.

We can see that due to the overall top-line weakness, the company’s margins were pressured as well. The easy explanation is the costs didn’t fall as fast as its revenue, and the mix was still largely affected by the declining wireless as well as growth in lower-margin services work. The company must have a lot more fixed costs on the books, so if sales suffer, there are fewer units to spread out those costs across. Margins across the board are down around 100bps from the highs set in the second quarter of the year.

And it is not surprising that other key efficiency metrics have followed suit. Since the second quarter, SPOK’s management has ended up utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital less efficiently. The decline isn’t particularly bad, but it is not a good trend. Also, these are below what I would consider decent. Especially ROE and ROTC. It seems like the company is losing pricing power, and its moat is deteriorating.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. SPOK finished the year with a little over $25m in cash and equivalents, against no debt, apart from some lease obligations. This is particularly good for a company that is experiencing slight inefficiencies in operations, like SPOK. A balance sheet like that can help the companies weather the uncertainty and operational issues a lot better than a balance sheet that has to allocate some of the resources to paying down interest on debt or the debt itself. Additionally, we can see that the company’s cash from operations actually bucked the trend we are seeing, as it has, more or less, recovered from Q3 lows.

Its free cash flow was around $25m for the year, which has been very consistent throughout the years. It’s not a bad sign, but it isn’t growing either.

Looking at the past year’s performance, the only thing I can say about SPOK is that this may not have been the year of change and transformation that anyone would have hoped. The management wasn’t able to successfully offset the decline in legacy wireless with its software segment, and that could be a larger issue at hand.

Comments on the Outlook

I think it is safe to say that SPOK needs to fully focus on getting its software adoption up a notch. What it is doing right now hardly makes for decent growth if its legacy wireless continues to decline at a faster pace than its software is growing. Wireless will continue to decline and eventually disappear. It’s structural erosion, something that just has to happen as more and more customers switch to software-based solutions that SPOK is offering. That means I am expecting wireless to be cannibalized by its software segment, as well as customers switching to another provider altogether. The bad thing about this trend, however, is that right now, wireless produces the bulk of operating cash flows relative to its non-growth. That is because this segment is already mature and requires low capex and upkeep. That means most of the revenue is essentially going to trickle down to the bottom line. Software is the growth driver, and it requires a lot of investment, which is going to put a damper on its cash generation for now. I am expecting to see cash from operations suffer in the future, until software investments slow down and the company reaches the inflection point, when the legacy declines won’t be as detrimental as they are right now.

If we dig a little deeper into the revenues of software, we can see that managed services did particularly well this past year, or over the last few years, for that matter. This part of the service that SPOK offers seems to be resonating with the hospitals quite a bit since the growth in revenue here was 134% from ’23 to ’24, and 103% from ’24 to ’25. Granted, it is still only around 10% of the total software revenue, but if SPOK can keep up the momentum, this is going to be a serious contender as the main growth driver. The managed services segment is basically Spok helping customers operate, configure, maintain, and optimize parts of the communication environment for the hospital/customer. No one wants to do this, especially if they are unfamiliar with the platform, and who else is better equipped to optimize if not SPOK themselves?

In order to succeed in this transition, the company needs to show that it can grow its software bookings as well as convert them into actual revenue in the future. The company seems to be doing fine for now, but what happens when it starts to slow down? SPOK won’t see a top-line growth for a while. Booking rose 83% sequentially, which is great. I’d like to see the momentum sustained, so the software business begins to properly offset the losses in wireless and revitalize its top-line performance.

Is SPOK worth an Investment?

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to see what the company might be worth to us. Below are the assumptions I used.

Revenue growth may be a little optimistic, given that it has been going down recently. Its 5-year CAGR was -0.35%. I’ll stick with 2%, nonetheless.

For margins, I am modeling an improvement overall because as legacy declines, software increases, margin mix improves due to that shift in economics, so it is reasonable to assume that profitability improves. It’s only an improvement of 60bps over the next half-decade, so I am being rather conservative here. The rest I am keeping close to the company’s historical averages for further simplicity. Additionally, as it is a small-cap company that is not very well covered, with low average trading volume, I have decided to use around 8% as my discount instead of the company’s WACC of 7% or so. That way I get a bit of margin of safety. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we get the company’s intrinsic value to be around $10.15 a share, which means SPOK is still trading a bit over its fair value, even after the recent poor stock performance.

Closing Comments

If you have been holding the company since it hit 52-week highs back in August of last year, then I don’t see the point in selling right now. I think the worst has already passed, and all of that is more or less already priced in. The company just needs to show it can successfully revitalize its operations through further software adoption for customers so that the legacy wireless doesn’t dampen the results quite so badly.

I would be looking to initiate a position somewhere in the 9$ range or at least revisit the thesis to see if there has been any fundamental change. But for now, I am going to continue to sit on the fence.

data.title: TurboQuant Fears Overblown, Micron Deserves A Buy
data.description:
Introduction:

What a week for memory. The market was absolutely stunned last week following the news that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) had devised a new algorithmic method, known as TurboQuant, which would reduce the memory requirement for AI workloads by up to a factor of 6. The news sparked a sell off in the big memory companies, including Micron (MU). The narrative behind the sell-off was this new algorithm would fundamentally change the paradigm for memory use in memory-heavy workloads, such as AI, with the end result being less memory demand and a big P&L hit for the likes of Micron.

I find it unlikely that AI developers are going to suddenly decide they need to use less memory. The reason there are memory shortages and bottlenecks at present is due to insatiable underlying demand. A step change in efficiency is a good thing; it means developers are able to achieve more with less, but I think in the arms race to develop the best systems, everybody will still want more. I see the sell-off in memory names as irrational, and I see Micron as a buy rating.

TurboQuant:

The first thing worth acknowledging about the TurboQuant breakthrough, if it's correct to call it that, is that it's a testament to the enduring power of innovation. It never ceases to amaze me the ability of engineers, technicians, and developers to innovate and push technology standards forward. Technology changes over time create winners and losers in the markets, but at the system level, it is ultimately a benefit to economies and markets.

The sell-off in memory stocks post the Google announcement strikes me as a classic knee-jerk market reaction that we see from time to time. The pattern is consistent: some market news comes out that challenges the existing wisdom. Market participants, which these days can often just be trading systems using natural language programs to read through news stories and headlines, see the news come out and react by trading positions immediately. This to me is not investing; it gives no thought or due consideration to the dynamics at play. A recent example of this behavior in the market was the SaaS sell off, which serves as an example of negative headlines for software being followed by indiscriminate selling of anything and everything software. Little thought was given to how much direct exposure firms actually hold.

The current memory situation, to me, looks like a carbon copy of what happened last year with DeepSeek. For those who don't remember, a Chinese AI lab called DeepSeek released a reasoning model called R1 which was developed using a fraction of the compute cost of the leading Western models at the time. Semiconductor stocks took a beating as the prevailing narrative was there would not be the same compute demand, as leading AI developers would be able to do more with less. So no need for incredibly expensive Nvidia (NVDA) Blackwell systems, right?

Exactly, we know how that affair played out. Jevon's Paradox proved to be very much a real thing, and the prevailing narrative shifted to, if we can do more with less, then let's just use more and do even more. Some may disagree with me, but I cannot see why the usage of memory would be any different from what we saw with GPUs last year. Google's algorithm may well have vastly improved the efficiency of memory for AI workloads. What do you think developers are going to do? I feel pretty sure they are going to utilize even more memory, leading to ever greater context windows.

Memory Backdrop:

The global memory market is in a huge supply/demand imbalance right now. The memory market is a classically cyclical market where supply and demand gyrate and create a consistent series of peaks and troughs over time. The AI story has pushed the market into a significant supply deficit at this moment. The key reason for this is the rapid growth of high-bandwidth memory, or HBM which requires up to 4x as many wafers as traditional DRAM. The big AI players, like the hyperscalers, cannot get their hands on enough memory at present, either for their own custom silicon or as an attachment to an Nvidia Blackwell compute rack.

In the past, memory procurement was done on a short term basis, but now, due to the unprecedented level of demand, customers are locking in future demand through longer-term contracts. This change in order patterns clearly signals where the market is at: demand is dominating supply. The longer term ordering also gives memory players unusual levels of visibility into their future revenue streams. Hock Tan, the CEO of Broadcom (AVGO) was noted recently as saying his firm had secured their memory supply as far out as 2028.

I would caution that any talk of an end to the cyclicality of memory should be taken with a pinch of salt. Memory has always followed a cycle, and I expect at some point in the future it will revert back to its natural pattern. What we are seeing right now is just a very unusual cycle with an incredible amount of demand supporting a sustained upside momentum. Given we see such a strong demand signal and multiyear contracts, I think this supports a buy thesis on Micron over the medium term.

Valuation

Micron's valuation vs. history is either incredibly attractive or incredibly unattractive, depending on what metric you apply. Why do I say that? Well, if we look at it on a forward P/E basis, as seen below, it looks like an easy pick. The current P/E is only 4x, and it's well below recent averages.

There is a catch, of course; the chart above is only a one-year P/E chart, and I chose that for good reason. Take a look at the long-term P/E chart below, which goes back to the mid-90s. As you can see, it's totally whacky; the P/E jumps all over the place. The reason for this is the extreme cyclical nature of the memory market, as I discussed above. Earnings in the industry swing between highs and lows; this makes it tough to use a price-to-earnings metric as a way to gauge the valuation of a company.

The classic way to value memory stocks is to employ a price-to-book approach. This approach strips out the volatility inherent in earnings and looks at the equity or book value of the company's balance sheet. Price-to-book is not without limitation in my mind; rather than looking ahead at earnings to come, it focuses on the accounting value of a firm, which is a backward-looking metric. It's not perfect, but it is much steadier. Based on this methodology, Micron is pretty obviously expensive.

Micron is expensive, but it has become pretty clear over the past year or so that the demand for memory is in a different paradigm compared to any other period in history. The unusual use of long-term contracts by customers signals a desperation to lock in supply, and Micron is essentially sold out for years to come. Hence I think the firm should trade at an expensive valuation; it's appropriate for the moment. I don't think the wave of demand will last forever in memory, but it does look set to continue for at least 3-5 years, and I think Micron stands to do very well during that time-frame.

Risks

Micron naturally plays a role in the AI ecosystem. As capex dollars flow out from the hyperscalers, they naturally land as revenue at companies such as Nvidia or the memory players like Micron. The revenue coming from the big hyperscaler capital spending has come to dominate the revenue and earnings outlook for Micron in a way that is not normal. Of course it isn't normal; we are living through the age of the initial AI buildout, a phase of incredible infrastructure spending, so it makes sense why this revenue stream has come to dominate other traditional streams such as memory for smartphones or PCs.

The question really is, at what point do the hyperscalers say we have enough infrastructure built, the peak has passed, and now we plan to rein in our capex in order to focus more on things like shareholder returns? Once that point is hit, I believe the revenue and earnings opportunity for a company like Micron could fundamentally change. I mentioned memory is a cyclical industry with peaks and troughs. The current trajectory is towards a very high peak, maybe the highest in the history of the industry, but what follows will naturally be a steep decline.

When will that point come? I don't know. What I do know is the memory market is reported to be completely sold out for years, and customers have been seeking to enter into longer-term contracts, something not typical of the industry. So that tells me the peak is likely still a while away. Given the uncertainty, though, I would advise readers that appropriate position sizing is key, Micron represents a great opportunity, and I think there is still room to run, but choose a position size that adequately balances the opportunity with the risk.

Conclusion

I think the market reaction to the Google TurboQuant news was a rinse and repeat of what we saw last year with DeepSeek. The market clearly hasn't learned the lessons of that episode. We saw clearly last year that the desire for compute for AI is insatiable, and that holds true for memory in my mind too. The memory demand is clearly evidenced by an industry that has capacity sold out, I do not think a change in memory efficiency will change the underlying level of demand one bit.

I feel pretty sure the leading AI labs want as much memory as they can possibly get a hold of, and they all know that's what their rivals think too, increasing the urgency. With this very strong demand backdrop, I think Micron has room to run over the medium term, and I see the pullback as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Can Any Investor Actually Value SpaceX?
data.description:
Why this IPO is historic:

SpaceX (SPACE) is back in the headlines again, and this time the catalyst is one to really look at. The company confidentially filed its draft IPO registration with the SEC on Wednesday, April 1st, putting it on track for what could be a June listing. If it prices near expectations, it will be the largest public offering in history.

SpaceX could seek a valuation of over $1.75 trillion, a number that would top every prior IPO and make it the first company to debut on public markets above the trillion-dollar mark. Even talks of a possible IPO sent aerospace players in the sector flying last year, as seen below.

Once again, the announcement is sending shockwaves across the aerospace sector, even as the broader market sells off on geopolitical tension.

The SpaceX IPO filing lifted the entire space basket on Thursday, with Planet Labs (PL) up 9.49%, Satellogic (SATL) up 8.06%, Viasat (VSAT) up 7.90%, Intuitive Machines (LUNR) up 6.87%, Echostar (SATS) up 2.55%, Firefly Aerospace (FLY) up 1.81%, Rocket Lab (RKLB) up 1.65%, and AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) up 1.36%.

To put the fundraising in perspective: the company is reportedly targeting up to $75 billion in proceeds, more than three times the size of Saudi Aramco's $29 billion debut in 2019, which has stood as the record for seven years.

This time around, though, SpaceX is entering the market considerably larger and more complex than the rocket business many investors originally tracked in private markets. SpaceX merged with Elon Musk's xAI (X.AI) earlier this year, creating a combined entity spanning aerospace, satellite internet, social media via X, and AI, with Musk having announced ambitions to deploy a network of one million orbital satellites serving as AI data centers. Revenue is approaching $20 billion in 2026, with the launch program and Starlink driving the bulk of it, while xAI is expected to contribute less than $1 billion in the near term.

What you’re buying:

SpaceX is considering a dual-class share structure that would give insiders, including Musk, extra voting power, and up to 30% of the offering could be allocated to retail investors. Note that the latter is an unusually large retail tranche, signaling that the company is deliberately cultivating a broad shareholder base from day one.

The launch business remains the brand anchor, mainly through reusable Falcon 9 rockets. The latter’s reusability program has fundamentally restructured the economics of getting to orbit. By recovering and reusing first-stage boosters, with one having now completed 34 missions as of early 2026, SpaceX has driven its internal launch costs down to somewhere in the $15 million to $30 million range per mission, against a comparable figure closer to $80 million for traditional competitors.

But launch is increasingly the infrastructure layer that funds everything else, not the growth story itself. The growth story is Starlink, and I’ll get to that in a second.

Now, let’s look at the numbers. The company’s revenue has been on a steep upward trajectory, from $13.1 billion in 2024 to roughly $15 billion in 2025, with analysts projecting a jump to nearly $24 billion in 2026, around 50% year-over-year growth. Starlink contributed about $10.4 billion in 2025, nearly 70% of total company revenue, and according to Payload, that number is expected to nearly double to $18.7 billion this year, which would push Starlink's share of consolidated revenue to roughly 80%.

The real, recurring revenue engine:

While the Falcon 9 program commands the most attention, the actual earnings story at SpaceX is Starlink. The latter crossed 10 million subscribers in February, adding its most recent million users in under 53 days. Starlink is now approaching 10,000 satellites in low-Earth orbit, serving millions of customers globally, with revenue scaling rapidly toward the majority of the company's top line.

That subscriber base now spans more than 155 countries, with the majority of customers located outside the U.S. Geographic diversification meaningfully reduces concentration risk for prospective public-market investors. Starlink's user base has been doubling roughly every year, from 2.3 million at end of 2023, to 4.6 million at end of 2024, to 9.2 million by end of 2025, and the consensus is now around 18.4 million by end of 2026.

What makes this particularly interesting from a cash flow perspective is the margin inflection underway. Gross margins that were in the low single digits just two years ago are expected to approach 25% in 2026, as the capital-intensive satellite buildout phase gives way to high-margin recurring subscription revenue.

The competitive moat deserves serious credit, too. Starlink's first-mover advantage in low-Earth orbit, along with its vertical integration advantage that no rival can replicate, as it launches its own satellites on its own rockets. Add the FCC's approval of an additional 7,500 second-generation satellites and a direct-to-cell capability that integrates with standard smartphones, and the addressable market expands far beyond, and into global mobile connectivity.

How to value SpaceX:

The first problem in valuing the stock is that there's no obvious ticker to benchmark it against. The company has eggs in 4 baskets, and they trade at wildly different multiples and attract entirely different investor bases.

If you frame SpaceX as an aerospace and defense name, the natural competitors are Boeing (BA) and Lockheed Martin (LMT), both of which trade at 1-2x revenue. At that multiple, SpaceX's projected 2026 revenue of $24 billion implies a valuation of around $30-50 billion, which would make the $1.75 trillion IPO target look absurdly huge, and it’s safe to say no one is using that framework. If you frame it as a telecom play anchored on Starlink's recurring subscription revenue, you'd look at satellite operators like Viasat (VSAT), which is up around 14% on Thursday alone, or traditional broadband providers, which trade at 2-4x revenue, still nowhere near justifying a $1.75 trillion valuation on current financials.

To defend this valuation, one must frame Starlink as a hypergrowth technology platform, closer to how the market valued early Netflix (NFLX) or Spotify (SPOT), where you're paying for future subscriber penetration and margin expansion. On that framework, a 70-80x forward revenue multiple becomes the conversation, which is roughly where the IPO valuation math lands.

Then there's the xAI layer, which introduces an entirely separate AI infrastructure narrative that’s loosely related to hyperscalers and GPU cloud plays. What I think will end up happening is that SpaceX will be valued as a Musk premium conglomerate, a sum-of-parts story.

Risks I’m watching:

For all the consumer-facing appeal of Starlink and the excitement around xAI, a meaningful portion of SpaceX's business is underwritten by the federal government, and that's a risk profile worth understanding before the IPO.

The company has collected over $24 billion in government contracts since 2008, spanning NASA cargo and crew missions, Space Force national security launches, and a Pentagon contract signed in April 2025 worth $5.9 billion for 28 national security launch missions through 2029. That revenue is predictable and high-margin, which is why it's been a core part of the bull case in the private markets.

The risk is that it's also inherently political. SpaceX's largest single customer is the U.S. government, and while Musk has stepped back from his formal role in DOGE, his political profile remains elevated, and his relationship with the current administration continues to carry weight, for better or worse.

Why I’m bullish nonetheless:

My bullish take on SpaceX rests on two things that are difficult to find in a single public equity: optionality and the absence of a credible single competitor.

What you're really buying here is several large markets in one stock. Starlink alone takes a lot of the weight, but layer on xAI's orbital data center ambitions, direct-to-cell mobile connectivity, and the long-term Starship economics, and you have a company that could look radically different, and radically larger, in five years than it does today. A company that would need to be beaten on four different fronts at once is, in practice, very difficult to disrupt.
